narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 36, just shy of the 36.7 average, your law school application stood out in several ways, including your upper-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 51.1% chance that you would fail it. This tipped the prediction toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with the lowest predicted risk. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a strong favorable influence by exceeding the norm and suggesting solid foundational skills. Your LSAT score also pulled in the opposite direction, contributing positively toward a passing prediction due to its near-average strength, which often correlates with bar success. Overall, while your test scores and prior GPA showed promise, the model weighed your weaker Year 3 decile most heavily, viewing it as the biggest red flag against bar passage in your specific case. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher decile rankings across the board, this gap in your later performance made your profile riskier in the model's eyes. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your strong metrics elsewhere, but this doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing targeted academic improvement to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-bac program or additional certifications that demonstrate upward trends. You might also retake the LSAT for a slight edge or gather letters emphasizing your resilience as an Asian male navigating competitive academics. With focused steps like these, you can strengthen your profile for the next application cycle and show the model—and schools—your true capability.
